title: HumorGen: Cognitive Synergy for Humor Generation in Large Language Models via Persona-Based Distillation
research_questions: Can a cognitively structured Mixture-of-Thought data-generation framework produce humor training data that lets a 7B student model compete with or outperform larger LLMs?
Does preference alignment with DPO or O-GRPO improve judged funniness beyond CSF-curated supervised fine-tuning?
Does training on persona-specific reasoning traces via Cognitive Synergy Distillation improve humor generation or cause an “explainer trap”?
Does a humor model trained from SemEval-style prompts transfer to out-of-distribution prompt domains in Humor Transfer Bench?
How reliable are LLM pairwise judges relative to human judgments for high-quality joke comparisons?

datasets_used.name: SemEval-2026 Task 1 MWAHAHA data
datasets_used.size: first 50 headlines from the official 300-prompt test set

datasets_used.name: Cognitive Synergy Framework synthetic candidate pool
datasets_used.size: ~28,800 candidates; Appendix reports 28,789 candidates across 1,200 prompts

datasets_used.name: O-GRPO Data (D_GRPO)
datasets_used.size: all 24 candidates per prompt across the 1,200 prompts; Table 10 O-GRPO pool N=28,789

methods_proposed.name: Cognitive Synergy Framework (CSF)
methods_proposed.summary: A Mixture-of-Thought humor data-generation framework that instantiates six cognitive personas—Neurotic, Cynic, Observer, Wordsmith, Optimist, and Absurdist—grounded in humor theories. For each prompt it generates diverse candidates, ranks them with pairwise LLM judging, and uses the resulting corpus for distillation.

methods_proposed.name: HumorGen
methods_proposed.summary: A family of 7B student models based on Qwen 2.5-7B-Instruct and fine-tuned on CSF-curated humor data. Variants include SFT, DPO, O-GRPO/GRPO, Think versions trained with reasoning traces, and a comedian-adapted model.

methods_proposed.name: Offline Group Relative Policy Optimization (O-GRPO)
methods_proposed.summary: An offline group-relative alignment variant that uses a fixed candidate pool instead of online sampling. It ranks all 24 candidates per prompt once, computes group-normalized Elo advantages, and trains with advantage-weighted SFT.

methods_proposed.name: Cognitive Synergy Distillation (CSD)
methods_proposed.summary: A process-distillation variant in which the student is trained on persona-specific reasoning traces inside `<think>` tags followed by the joke. At inference, reasoning is stripped before evaluation to compare against non-CSD models.

methods_proposed.name: Humor Transfer Bench (HTB)
methods_proposed.summary: A 400-prompt benchmark with eight prompt domains designed to test transfer in textual humor generation beyond news-headline prompts.

humor_theories.detail: The Neurotic persona is grounded in Relief Theory and focuses on tension release through internal anxiety, overthinking, and social insecurity.
The Cynic persona is grounded in Superiority Theory and focuses on social critique, hypocrisy, biting sarcasm, and moral contradictions.
The Observer and Absurdist personas are grounded in Incongruity Theory; Observer targets mundane social mismatches, while Absurdist targets surrealism, non-sequiturs, and fractured causality.
The Wordsmith persona is grounded in linguistic theories including Raskin’s Semantic Script Theory of Humor, using ambiguity, phonological similarity, double meanings, puns, and wordplay.
The Optimist persona is grounded in Benign Violation Theory and uses wholesome recontextualization or positive reinterpretation of potentially negative situations.

evaluation_methods.detail: Automated pairwise evaluation using Llama-3.3-70B and Qwen 2.5-72B judges; the judge selects the funnier joke or tie with randomized presentation order. The paper reports 42,000 pairwise comparisons for HTB and 5,250 for SemEval.

Pairwise outcomes are aggregated into a contest matrix and fit with a Bradley-Terry model via the MM algorithm; results are reported as Elo-scale BT ratings with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals using 100 bootstrap samples. Cross-judge stability is measured with Kendall tau.

Three blinded human evaluators judged 60 curated pairwise comparisons across 12 categories over 50 held-out headlines; the main text reports 170 blind pairwise judgments, while Appendix K reports 180 votes (3 evaluators, 60 pairs each). Metrics include inter-annotator agreement, Krippendorff’s alpha, LLM–human consensus, and micro-average agreement.

headline_results.finding: On HTB under the Llama 3.3-70B judge, HumorGen-SFT-7B ranked 3rd and HumorGen-DPO-7B ranked 4th, ahead of Gemini-2.5-Pro, GPT-OSS-120B, and Qwen3-32B.
HTB rankings were robust across the two LLM judges, with Qwen 2.5-72B placing HumorGen-DPO-Think-7B 3rd, HumorGen-DPO-7B 4th, and HumorGen-SFT-7B 5th.
On the SemEval MWAHAHA test subset under the Llama judge, HumorGen-SFT-7B and HumorGen-DPO-7B ranked 4th and 5th behind the three frontier systems and ahead of open-weight general models.
On SemEval under the Qwen judge, HumorGen-DPO-7B rose to 3rd and HumorGen-SFT-7B to 4th, both ahead of Gemini-2.5-Pro.
Preference alignment did not consistently improve over SFT; DPO and SFT had overlapping confidence intervals, while O-GRPO underperformed.
headline_results.value: HTB Llama: SFT 1128.14 vs DPO 1123.72 vs O-GRPO 1071.13; HTB Qwen: DPO 1138.05 vs SFT 1132.41; median advantage = −0.479
headline_results.best_system: HumorGen-SFT-7B under Llama; HumorGen-DPO-7B under Qwen

headline_results.finding: Human validation showed substantial subjectivity but the LLM judge matched human majority preferences above chance in the paper’s “Good vs. Good” regime.
headline_results.metric: human agreement; Krippendorff’s alpha; LLM-human agreement
headline_results.value: Human agreement 31.7%; Krippendorff’s alpha α = 0.425 across three evaluators and α = 0.412 across two; LLM judge matched human consensus on 58.3% of pairs and individual votes at 52.4%

key_findings: CSF-curated synthetic data enabled 7B HumorGen variants to outperform substantially larger open-weight/general baselines on HTB and SemEval while remaining behind GPT-5 and Kimi-K2 in most leaderboards.
DPO did not yield a statistically meaningful improvement over SFT in the main evaluations because their BT confidence intervals overlapped across benchmarks and judges.
O-GRPO consistently underperformed SFT and DPO; the paper attributes this to a right-skewed advantage distribution with median advantage = −0.479.

Think/CSD variants usually trailed their non-thinking counterparts, supporting the paper’s “explainer trap” claim that reasoning traces can bias outputs toward explaining rather than delivering jokes.
Per-persona analysis found Neurotic strongest and Wordsmith weakest: Neurotic had 63.4% pairwise win rate, mean rank 9.3, and top-3 rate 26.6%, while Wordsmith had 34.9%, 16.1, and 3.1%.

Fine-tuning HumorGen-SFT-7B on 998 Shaun Eli stand-up jokes sharply regressed performance from BT 1083.9 to 653.1, which the authors attribute to mismatch between performance-native stand-up and text-native joke generation.
limitations: The study is limited to English, text-only humor.
Evaluation is limited to two benchmarks: SemEval-MWAHAHA and Humor Transfer Bench.
Human evaluation uses a small annotator pool (N = 3) over 60 sampled pairwise comparisons, limiting statistical coverage.
Model comparisons are restricted to 15 selected models due to compute constraints.
The paper notes that humor generation can produce jokes that are offensive to some listeners or culturally insensitive.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper states that humor generation risks producing offensive content and that the framework encourages wordplay and absurdity over denigration or prejudice. Persona prompts include safety constraints against racism, sexism, slurs, and punching down at vulnerable groups. Human evaluators were volunteers recruited by invitation and were not paid; the authors state that all content was written and verified by them and that LLM assistance was used only for grammar correction, language polishing, and LaTeX formatting.

artifacts.other: HTB-authored prompts are described as released under CC BY 4.0, but no repository or download URL is stated in the paper.

human_samples.population: Master’s-level students in Computer Science or Artificial Intelligence
human_samples.recruitment: Initial qualification pool; candidates completed a qualification task with humor identification and pairwise joke ranking on a shared example set.
human_samples.observations: Qualification-task results were used to select the three retained annotators; detailed qualification results are not reported.
human_samples.detail: Appendix K states that the three main annotators were selected from this pool based on inter-annotator agreement and response consistency. The 12 includes the retained annotators, not an additional main-evaluation sample.

human_samples.population: Master’s-level students in Computer Science or Artificial Intelligence
human_samples.recruitment: Volunteers recruited by invitation; selected from the qualification pool; no compensation was provided.
human_samples.observations: 60 curated pairwise comparisons across 12 categories over 50 held-out headlines. Main text reports 170 blind pairwise judgments; Appendix K reports 180 votes (3 evaluators × 60 pairs each).
human_samples.detail: Evaluators signed in via Google, saw a headline and two anonymous jokes, and voted for the funnier one. Model identities were hidden and A/B order was randomized. Reported metrics: human unanimous agreement 31.7%, Krippendorff’s α = 0.425 for three evaluators and α = 0.412 for two, LLM–human majority agreement 58.3%, micro-average agreement 52.4%.

judges.name: Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct
judges.detail: Used to judge and rank the full CSF candidate pool for training-data construction; used as an automated pairwise judge for HTB and SemEval leaderboards; also assigns structured winner/loser tags in the HTB analysis. The paper reports randomized A/B presentation but does not explicitly state model-identity blinding for the LLM judge.

judges.name: Qwen 2.5-72B
judges.detail: Used as a second automated pairwise judge for both HTB and SemEval to test cross-judge robustness. Reported Kendall τ vs. the Llama judge: 0.8667 on HTB and 0.8286 on SemEval.
Three retained human evaluators judged the curated 60-pair validation set. They were blind to model identity; candidate order was randomized.

judges.name: Bradley-Terry / Elo ratings via MM algorithm
judges.detail: Pairwise judge outcomes were aggregated into contest matrices and fit with a Bradley-Terry model; Elo-scale BT ratings with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals from 100 bootstrap samples determined leaderboard rankings.

judges.name: Kendall rank correlation
judges.detail: Used to compare model rankings produced by the Llama and Qwen LLM judges. Exact BT ties were broken deterministically by model-name order before rank correlation was computed.

generation_selection.scope: CSF training-data synthesis and curation from the 1,200 SemEval experimental prompts
generation_selection.n: 24 candidates per prompt (4 per persona × 6 personas); paper reports ~28,800 raw candidates and Appendix tables report 28,789 actual candidates
generation_selection.detail: A teacher ensemble of Kimi-K2 and Qwen 2.5-32B-Instruct generated the candidate pool. Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct judged pairwise tournaments and Elo/BT rankings drove selection: SFT used the top 10 per prompt, DPO randomly paired top-5 candidates as chosen with bottom-5 candidates as rejected, and O-GRPO retained all 24 candidates with group-relative weights.

generation_selection.scope: Final model-output generation for HTB and SemEval automated leaderboards
generation_selection.detail: The paper reports pairwise comparisons among 15 models over fixed benchmark prompts, but does not state decoding parameters, whether each model produced exactly one sampled output per prompt, or whether any best-of-N generation/selection was used before judging.

Think variants generate reasoning followed by the joke; the reasoning is stripped before evaluation. The paper does not report the number of samples, decoding parameters, or any selection among multiple generations.

model_provenance.name: Kimi-K2
model_provenance.detail: Used in the CSF teacher ensemble and evaluated as a frontier baseline. No endpoint, snapshot, revision, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: Qwen 2.5-32B-Instruct
model_provenance.detail: Used in the CSF teacher ensemble; the human-evaluation table also refers to a 32B teacher comparison. No revision or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct
model_provenance.detail: Actually run as the main LLM judge for data curation and leaderboard evaluation. No model revision, serving route, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: Qwen 2.5-72B
model_provenance.detail: Actually run as the second LLM judge. The paper does not specify whether this is an instruct/chat checkpoint, nor any revision or inference date.

model_provenance.name: Qwen 2.5-7B-Instruct / Base Qwen-7B
model_provenance.detail: Reported as the base architecture for all HumorGen fine-tuning and evaluated as Base Qwen-7B. Fine-tuning used NVIDIA H100-80GB GPUs, but no checkpoint revision is reported.

model_provenance.name: HumorGen-SFT-7B
model_provenance.version_or_snapshot: LoRA fine-tuned from Qwen 2.5-7B-Instruct on D_SFT (top-10 CSF candidates per prompt; N=12,000)
model_provenance.detail: Own trained model variant; training hyperparameters are reported, but no checkpoint hash/repository or inference date is given.

model_provenance.name: HumorGen-DPO-7B
model_provenance.version_or_snapshot: DPO-aligned HumorGen variant using D_DPO (N=6,000 preference pairs)
model_provenance.detail: Own trained model variant initialized from the SFT pipeline; no checkpoint hash/repository or inference date is given.

model_provenance.name: HumorGen-GRPO-7B
model_provenance.version_or_snapshot: O-GRPO / GRPO variant using all ranked CSF candidates with group-relative advantages
model_provenance.detail: Leaderboards call this GRPO although the method is described as offline O-GRPO. No checkpoint hash/repository or inference date is given.

model_provenance.name: HumorGen-SFT-Think-7B
model_provenance.version_or_snapshot: CSD Think variant trained with persona reasoning traces plus jokes under SFT settings
model_provenance.detail: Reasoning traces are generated at inference and stripped before evaluation. No checkpoint hash/repository or inference date is given.

model_provenance.name: HumorGen-DPO-Think-7B
model_provenance.version_or_snapshot: CSD Think variant trained with reasoning traces in DPO chosen/rejected responses
model_provenance.detail: No checkpoint hash/repository or inference date is given.

model_provenance.name: HumorGen-GRPO-Think-7B
model_provenance.version_or_snapshot: CSD Think variant trained with O-GRPO/GRPO settings
model_provenance.detail: No checkpoint hash/repository or inference date is given.

model_provenance.name: HumorGen-Com-7B
model_provenance.version_or_snapshot: HumorGen-SFT-7B further fine-tuned on 998 Shaun Eli stand-up jokes
model_provenance.detail: Section 5.2 calls this HumorGen-Com-7B; Table 13 appears to typo it as HumorGen-Con-7B. No checkpoint hash/repository or inference date is given.

model_provenance.name: GPT-5
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a frontier system on the automated leaderboards. No API model string, snapshot, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: Gemini-2.5-Pro
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a frontier/proprietary baseline. No API model string, snapshot, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: Qwen3-32B
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as an open-weight general baseline. No checkpoint revision or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT-OSS-120B
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a large baseline. No checkpoint revision/snapshot or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: phi2-Humor
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a humor-specialized baseline; cited as a SemEval 2026 humor-generation checkpoint.

model_provenance.name: JokeGPT
model_provenance.version_or_snapshot: TzJ2006 JokeGPT-Model; PPO-tuned LoRA adapter on Qwen3-8B; no revision reported

model_provenance.name: GPT-4o
model_provenance.detail: Appears in the human-evaluation category design as an SFT-7B vs GPT-4o comparison, but is not part of the 15-model automated leaderboards. No API model string, snapshot, or inference date is reported.

inference_budgets.scope: CSF candidate generation for training data
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 24 candidates per source prompt (4 per persona × 6 personas) across 1,200 prompts; raw pool reported as ~28,800 and Appendix as 28,789
inference_budgets.detail: Generated from a teacher ensemble of Kimi-K2 and Qwen 2.5-32B-Instruct. The paper does not report per-teacher allocation, number of API/model calls, decoding parameters, or max generation tokens.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Complete pairwise tournament per prompt over the 24 candidates; implementation-level call count not reported
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Judge selects funnier joke or tie; presentation order randomized. Judge decoding parameters are not reported.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Candidate ranking for the full pool consumed ~132 H100 node-hours.
inference_budgets.detail: Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct pairwise judgments produced Elo/BT rankings used for SFT, DPO, and O-GRPO data.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: 42,000 pairwise comparisons per judge over 400 prompts and 15 models
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 15 model outputs per prompt are compared pairwise; number of generated samples per model/prompt is not reported
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: A/B presentation order randomized for judging; model-output decoding parameters not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Both Llama-3.3-70B and Qwen 2.5-72B judged HTB. BT ratings used 100 bootstrap samples for confidence intervals.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: 5,250 pairwise comparisons per judge over the first 50 official test headlines and 15 models
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 15 model outputs per prompt are compared pairwise; number of generated samples per model/prompt is not reported
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: A/B presentation order randomized for judging; model-output decoding parameters not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Both Llama-3.3-70B and Qwen 2.5-72B judged SemEval. BT ratings used 100 bootstrap samples for confidence intervals.

inference_budgets.token_budget: Max sequence length 1024 for SFT/SFT-Think, DPO/DPO-Think, and GRPO/GRPO-Think; DPO prompt max 512.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Trained on NVIDIA H100-80GB GPUs using LoRA r=16. Reported runtimes: SFT 7.2m, SFT-Think 7.8m, DPO 18.5m, DPO-Think 21.2m, GRPO 23.7m, GRPO-Think 6.0h, HumorGen-Con/Com 2.3m.
inference_budgets.detail: SFT configured for 3 epochs; DPO and O-GRPO configured for 5 epochs with early stopping patience=2. Table 12 reports learning rates, batch size 16, optimizers, bf16 precision, and alignment-specific settings β=0.1 for DPO and G=24, T=1.0 for GRPO.

inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Runtime reported as 2.3m in Table 13; trained on 998 Shaun Eli jokes.
inference_budgets.detail: Learning rate 5e-5 with cosine schedule, batch size 16, 2 epochs, warmup ratio 0.05, AdamW 8-bit, base checkpoint HumorGen-SFT-7B.

human_baselines.description: No prompt-matched human-written joke baseline is evaluated against model outputs on the same prompts. Humans only serve as evaluators in the validation study.
human_baselines.detail: The main comparisons are among LLM-generated outputs judged by LLMs, with a small human validation subset.

human_baselines.description: The paper uses 998 human-written Shaun Eli stand-up jokes as fine-tuning data for HumorGen-Com-7B, but the human jokes themselves are not evaluated as a prompt-matched output baseline.
human_baselines.detail: The reported regression is for the model after fine-tuning on the stand-up corpus, not a direct comparison between human and model jokes under matched generation/evaluation conditions.

mitigations: HTB domains A–G are described as HTB-authored prompts and HTB is evaluation-only in this paper.
The paper states that no model is fine-tuned on HTB data.
SemEval evaluation uses the first 50 prompts from the official 300-prompt test set, while training uses a separate 1,200-prompt SemEval experimental set as described.
contamination_risk.basis: Risk is mixed. HTB-authored prompts and the no-fine-tuning-on-HTB statement reduce direct training leakage for the authors’ models, but Domain H uses publicly available BBC headlines, SemEval test prompts are official benchmark items, and the paper reports no decontamination test or pretraining-exposure analysis for GPT-5, Kimi-K2, Gemini-2.5-Pro, GPT-4o, open-weight baselines, or the LLM judges. Model snapshots and inference dates are also not reported, so public-data exposure cannot be ruled out.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: Leaderboard rankings on HTB and SemEval; claims that 7B HumorGen variants outperform larger baselines; SFT vs DPO vs O-GRPO conclusions; CSD/Think explainer-trap conclusions; persona-performance analysis; judge-assigned mechanism/flaw tag analysis; and the training-data selection used to create SFT/DPO/O-GRPO corpora.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Small validation study with 3 evaluators over 60 curated pairs; main text reports 170 judgments while Appendix K reports 180 votes. Human majority agreement with the LLM judge is 58.3%, micro-average 52.4%, with human unanimous agreement 31.7%.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: The headline empirical results rest mainly on LLM pairwise judgments and BT aggregation. Human evaluation is post-hoc and small, so it corroborates judge behavior only partially rather than independently determining the main conclusions.

reporting_gaps: No API model strings, checkpoint revisions, snapshots, or inference dates are reported for proprietary/frontier systems, LLM judges, or most open-weight baselines.
Access route is not specified for most external models and judges (API vs local vs hosted service).

Model-output generation settings for benchmark evaluation are not reported: temperature, top-p/top-k, max output tokens, stop sequences, random seeds, and whether outputs were sampled once or selected from multiple candidates.
The paper does not explicitly disclose any best-of-N or no-best-of-N policy for final benchmark outputs.
Teacher-ensemble allocation for the 24 CSF candidates is not specified: how many candidates came from Kimi-K2 vs Qwen 2.5-32B-Instruct is unclear.
Candidate-pool size is inconsistent/underexplained: the method describes 1,200 × 24 = 28,800 candidates, while Appendix tables report 28,789 actual candidates.
Human-evaluation counts conflict: main text reports 170 blind pairwise judgments, whereas Appendix K reports 180 votes from 3 evaluators × 60 pairs; no explanation is provided for the discrepancy.
Human evaluator demographics are sparse: no age, gender, nationality/cultural background, native language, or English proficiency is reported.
Human-evaluation pair curation is only summarized by category; the exact sampling/selection procedure for the 60 curated pairs is not fully specified.
No decontamination or memorization analysis is reported for SemEval, BBC headline prompts, HTB prompts, evaluated models, or LLM judges.
No code, checkpoint, or data repository URL is provided in the paper, despite stating HTB-authored prompts are released under CC BY 4.0.
LLM judge prompt details, tie handling in the contest matrix, and judge decoding/token budgets are not fully reported.
API costs or monetary cost estimates are not reported; only H100 node-hours for candidate ranking and training runtimes are given.
